name: Spoon Bending
description: Spoon bending, also known as psychokinesis or telekinesis applied to metal, is the purported paranormal ability to bend metal objects, typically spoons or forks, without the application of physical force. Proponents claim this is achieved through mental power or psychic energy. The phenomenon gained widespread attention in the 1970s with Uri Geller, who demonstrated spoon bending on television and became a prominent figure in the parapsychology movement. Geller attributed his abilities to psychic powers, often invoking extraterrestrial sources. Other proponents suggest that spoon bending demonstrates the latent potential of the human mind and its capacity to interact with the physical world in ways not currently understood by mainstream science. They often cite demonstrations conducted in controlled environments, sometimes involving children, as evidence of the phenomenon. However, the mainstream scientific perspective is highly skeptical. Scientists and magicians have demonstrated various techniques to replicate spoon bending effects through trickery, sleight of hand, and subtle physical manipulation. Critics argue that apparent demonstrations often occur in conditions that are not rigorously controlled, allowing for the possibility of deception. Furthermore, the lack of a consistent, repeatable, and scientifically verifiable mechanism for psychokinesis leads the vast majority of scientists to dismiss spoon bending as either fraudulent or a result of suggestion, misinterpretation, or unconscious physical force. Studies have also examined the role of social pressure and suggestion in influencing participants to believe they have witnessed or even performed spoon bending.
subtitle: Psychic metal bending: Fact or fiction?

claims.claim: Metal can be bent using only mental power.
claims.evidence: Demonstrations by individuals like Uri Geller and others.
claims.criticism: Demonstrations are often performed under uncontrolled conditions, allowing for trickery and sleight of hand. No scientifically validated mechanism exists.

claims.claim: Spoon bending is evidence of psychokinesis.
claims.evidence: Observed deformation of metal objects without apparent physical force.
claims.criticism: Alternative explanations such as subtle pressure, heat application, or pre-existing weaknesses in the metal are often overlooked. Confirmation bias and the observer effect contribute to misinterpretations.

claims.claim: Spoon bending demonstrates the potential of the human mind.
claims.evidence: Some proponents argue it shows untapped psychic abilities.
claims.criticism: The burden of proof lies with those making extraordinary claims. No robust scientific evidence supports the existence of psychic abilities. Alternative explanations, including suggestion and unconscious actions, are more parsimonious.

keyFigures.name: James Randi
keyFigures.role: Magician and skeptic who debunked many spoon bending claims.

mythologyConnections: Shamanistic practices involving manipulation of objects
Ancient beliefs in mind over matter

timeline.event: Uri Geller gains international fame for spoon bending demonstrations.
Skeptics begin to expose Geller's techniques as trickery.
Continued debate and investigation into spoon bending and psychokinesis.

sources.author: Targ, R., & Puthoff, H.
sources.work: Mind-Reach: Scientists Look at Psychic Ability
sources.title: Targ, R., & Puthoff, H.
